subject: Chemistry
title: The Maillard Reaction: Why Food Browns
description: A micro-lesson that makes The Maillard Reaction: Why Food Browns usable.

hook.text: That golden crust on toast, seared steak, and roasted coffee all come from the same chemical reaction.

buildup.text: When sugars and amino acids are heated above 140°C, they react to create hundreds of new flavor compounds.

discovery.text: Louis-Camille Maillard described this in 1912. It's not caramelization—it requires both sugar and protein.

twist.text: The reaction produces over 1,000 different molecules. That's why cooked food is so much more complex than raw.

climax.text: Chefs manipulate it by controlling heat, pH, and moisture. The Maillard reaction is cooking's secret weapon.

punchline.text: Sugar plus protein plus heat. That's the flavor of cooking.

quiz.question: What two ingredients does the Maillard reaction require?
